coredata.dc:description: Wound healing, particularly in the presence of bacterial infections, remains a significant challenge in clinical settings. This study explores the synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), and chitosan (CS), designated as CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites. The primary aim is to evaluate its wound healing efficacy and antibacterial activity. An in vivo incisional wound model in mice is utilized alongside in vitro assessments against clinically relevant Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) and Gram-negative (Escherichia coli, Acinetobacter baumannii, and Klebsiella pneumoniae) bacteria strains. The nanocomposites are characterized using X-ray diffraction, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, zeta potential analysis, and Scanning electron microscopy to confirm its structural integrity and functional properties. Preliminary results indicate that the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites significantly enhance wound healing and demonstrate potent antibacterial activity, with minimum bactericidal concentrations and minimum inhibitory concentrations ranging from 0.234 to 7.5 mg/mL. Histological analysis indicates that wounds treated with the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites exhibit accelerated re-epithelialization, improved wound repair, and a reduced inflammatory response, as evidenced by decreased neutrophil counts in the skin tissue, compared to untreated wounds and those treated with individual components (CQDs, AgNPs, CS) as well as the CQDs@CS composite. Notably, the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites also display impressive antioxidant activity, achieving an 80.21 % radical scavenging capacity against DPPH•. These findings suggest that CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites hold great promise for advanced wound care strategies, effectively integrating healing efficacy and infection control into a single formulation.

(A) Representative culture plates showcasing the diameters of inhibition zones produced by synthesized materials, and (B) representation of antibacterial activity mediated by CQDs (a), AgNPs (b), CQDs@CS (c), and CQDs@AgNPs@CS-based nanocomposites (d).

Schematic representation of the proposed mechanism by which CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites induce intracellular ROS production and oxidative damage in bacteria. (1) Membrane Interaction: Attachment of the positively charged CQDs@AgNPs@CS to the negatively charged bacterial cell surface via electrostatic interactions; (2) Protein Interaction: Binding to sulfhydryl groups of proteins; (3) Cellular Entry: Penetration of the nanocomposites into the cell, leading to oxidative stress and subsequent cell death; and (4) DNA Damage.

head.ce:title: Enhanced antibacterial and wound healing efficacy of a novel CQDs@AgNPs@CS-based nanocomposites: A multifunctional approach for advanced wound care

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wound healing, particularly in the presence of bacterial infections, remains a significant challenge in clinical settings. This study explores the synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), and chitosan (CS), designated as CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites. The primary aim is to evaluate its wound healing efficacy and antibacterial activity. An in vivo incisional wound model in mice is utilized alongside in vitro assessments against clinically relevant Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) and Gram-negative (Escherichia coli, Acinetobacter baumannii, and Klebsiella pneumoniae) bacteria strains. The nanocomposites are characterized using X-ray diffraction, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, zeta potential analysis, and Scanning electron microscopy to confirm its structural integrity and functional properties. Preliminary results indicate that the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites significantly enhance wound healing and demonstrate potent antibacterial activity, with minimum bactericidal concentrations and minimum inhibitory concentrations ranging from 0.234 to 7.5 mg/mL. Histological analysis indicates that wounds treated with the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites exhibit accelerated re-epithelialization, improved wound repair, and a reduced inflammatory response, as evidenced by decreased neutrophil counts in the skin tissue, compared to untreated wounds and those treated with individual components (CQDs, AgNPs, CS) as well as the CQDs@CS composite. Notably, the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites also display impressive antioxidant activity, achieving an 80.21 % radical scavenging capacity against DPPH•. These findings suggest that CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites hold great promise for advanced wound care strategies, effectively integrating healing efficacy and infection control into a single formulation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The skin is a vital organ of the human body [1], accounting for approximately 16 % of body weight and covering an average surface area of 1.85 square meters [2]. It performs critical functions, including serving as a barrier against environmental microorganisms, preventing dehydration, and protecting underlying tissues and organs from physical, mechanical, and chemical damage. When the skin's integrity is compromised due to injury, surgery, or disease physiological imbalances can occur, potentially leading to severe disability or even mortality [3]. Globally, an estimated 8.2 million individuals suffer from wounds—with or without infections—resulting in medical costs ranging from USD 28.1 billion to USD 96.8 billion [4].

The wound healing process has been extensively studied and is characterized as a complex biological sequence occurring in all tissues and organs [3,5,6]. This process involves various cell types, including keratinocytes, neutrophils, macrophages, lymphocytes, fibroblasts, and endothelial cells [7]. It begins hemostasis at the injury site, followed by an inflammatory phase, re-epithelialization, and matrix component proliferation, culminating in scar through the deposition of a highly organized collagen matrix [7].

Bacterial infections present a significant challenge in wound care by delaying the healing process and increasing the risk of complications, including hospitalization and, in severe cases, amputation. These infections can contribute to the chronic wounds, making effective management essential for improving patient outcomes [8]. The moist microenvironment surrounding wounds is particularly conducive to bacterial colonization, resulting in inflammation and further hindrance to the healing [9]. Consequently, an ideal therapeutic approach should promote rapid healing by accelerating wound contraction, reducing epithelialization time, and enhancing tensile strength, while also offering anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and anti-scarring properties [4].

Nanomaterials have emerged as promising candidates for tissue engineering and wound healing applications [10]. Various types, including metallic nanoparticles (NPs), polymeric NPs, nanoemulsions, solid lipid NPs, nanospheres, nanogels, and carbon-based nanocomposites—such as graphene, graphene oxide (GO), reduced GO (rGO), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), nanodiamonds, fullerenes, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs)—have demonstrated significant potential in wound healing and antibacterial activity research [11–15]. Among these, carbon nanomaterials stand out due to their excellent biocompatibility and distinctive antibacterial properties [15]. In particular, CQDs, a novel class of zero-dimensional carbon materials, possess advantageous characteristics such as ease of surface modification, higher solubility, lower toxicity, and easier synthesis, making them highly useful and a promising candidate for biomedical applications [16,17]. They also exhibit excellent optical and catalytic properties, as well as notable biocompatibility, making them promising candidates for antibacterial treatment [18]. Additionally, CQDs have gained increasing attention for their role in scavenging reactive oxygen species (ROS) and free radicals [18]. Recent studies have highlighted the development of CQD-based nanocomposites with antioxidant activity and their potential applications in treating inflammatory diseases [19,20]. Therefore, the synthesis of CQD-based nanocomposites with both antibacterial and antioxidative properties represents a promising strategy for wound healing applications.

Various materials have been used to modify CQDs, including metal NPs, conductive polymers, and biodegradable materials. Metal-based NPs, such as gold, zinc oxide NPs, silver (AgNPs), and copper oxide NPs, have gained significant traction in biomedical applications due to their optical, catalytic, and antibacterial properties, as well as their high surface area and low toxicity [21–25]. Among these, AgNPs exhibit broad-spectrum antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [26,27]. They function through multiple mechanisms, including bacterial membrane penetration, DNA damage, and the induction of ROS, which disrupt cellular metabolism [28]. Furthermore, the synergistic combination of AgNPs with other materials enhances their antibacterial activity, reduces particle size, and prevents aggregation [26]. The use of biodegradable materials offers additional opportunities for modifying AgNPs, improving their stability and functionality [29,30]. Various biodegradable materials, such as chitosan (CS)/gelatin [31], chitin/silk fibroin [32,33], alginate [34], and cellulose [35], have been explored for this purpose. Among these, CS—an amino polysaccharide derived from crustacean shells—is a particularly noteworthy biopolymer due to its renewable, non-toxic, biocompatible, and biodegradable nature [29,36]. Additionally, CS-based materials also exhibit controlled drug release mechanisms that depend on temperature and pH, demonstrating a dual-responsive release profile [8].

This study introduces a novel approach by synthesizing CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites, which uniquely harness the synergistic properties of CQDs, AgNPs, and CS. This innovative formulation is specifically designed to enhance wound healing efficacy while exhibiting potent antibacterial activity, distinguishing it from existing materials in the field. Building on previous research that examined individual components—often limited in effectiveness—our work provides a comprehensive solution for infection control and tissue regeneration, filling a crucial gap in the literature and paving the way for future multi-functional wound care therapies. The synthesized CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), UV–visible spectroscopy, and zeta potential analysis to elucidate their structural and functional properties. The primary objective of this study was to evaluate the wound healing performance of the synthesized nanocomposites using an in vivo incisional wound model in mice, complemented by comprehensive in vitro assessments against clinically relevant Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial strains. To achieve this, a multifaceted approach was employed, including detailed macro- and microscopic analysis of wound contraction in treated lesions. Preliminary results demonstrated that the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites exhibited significant wound healing and antibacterial efficacy against key pathogens, including Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). Escherichia coli (E. coli), Acinetobacter baumannii (A. baumannii), and Klebsiella pneumonia (K. pneumonia). The nanocomposites displayed minimum bactericidal concentrations (MBC) and minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) ranging from 0.234 to 7.5 mg/mL, underscoring their potent antibacterial properties. Notably, these nanocomposites outperformed individual components (CQDs, AgNPs, and CS) as well as the CQDs@CS nanocomposite, indicating their potential as a superior therapeutic agent in wound care applications. The findings of this study may pave the way for innovative treatments that effectively address the challenges of wound healing and infection management in clinical settings, while also contributing to several Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Specifically, this research supports SDG 3: Good Health and Well-Being by enhancing wound healing efficacy and reducing infections. Additionally, the innovative synthesis of CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites aligns with SDG 9: Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure, promoting advancements in materials science and sustainable industrial practices. Furthermore, this study contributes to SDG 12: Responsible Consumption and Production by developing multifunctional materials that prioritize efficient resource utilization, ultimately fostering sustainable production methods in healthcare.

Watermelon fruits were procured from the local store close to the faculty of sciences in Tetouan, Morocco. Silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.8–100.5 %), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, ≥ 96 %), commercial CS, acetic acid (C2H4O2, 100,5 %), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), and diethyl ether were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany).

A 250 mL stainless steel Teflon-lined autoclave was employed for the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs. The crystalline structures of the AgNPs, CQDs, CS, CQDS@CS, and CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites were analyzed using XRD patterns at ambient temperature. XRD analysis was conducted on a BRUKER-binary V4 X-ray diffractometer using CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Ǻ). Data were collected in continuous mode with Bragg Brentano geometry over a 2θ range from 15° to 65°. The XRD instrument was operated at 40 kV and 25 mA. The zeta potential of the samples was measured using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS. SEM analysis was employed to examine the surface morphology of the nanostructures using the Zeiss Sigma 500 VP Analytical FE-SEM (Carl Zeiss Company, Germany), while FTIR spectroscopy was performed using the Vertex 70 Bruker equipment for Attenuated Total Reflectance FTIR to analyze the chemical structure of the prepared materials. The UV–vis spectra were recorded with the Thermo Scientific™ Spectrophotometer UV–Visible Evolution™ 260 Bio, allowing for the identification of transition bonds associated with various functional groups.

CQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method with dried watermelon peels as the carbon source [37]. The process began by collecting watermelon peels, cutting them into smaller pieces, and thoroughly washing them with distilled water. The cleaned peels were then dried in an oven at 65 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, 1.2 g of the dried watermelon peel was mixed with 120 mL of distilled water, and the mixture was transferred into a 250 mL Teflon-lined-autoclave. The autoclave was heated at 200 °C for 6 h in an oven to facilitate the hydrothermal synthesis. After the reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature. The resulting brownish yellow solution was filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane filter and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min to remove any undissolved particles. The black solid precipitate was discarded, while the supernatant was collected and stored for further characterization and use. Upon exposure to UV irradiation, the color of the product changed to a blue photoluminescence, indicating that the quantum dot particles were successfully synthesized.

AgNPs were synthesized using a simple chemical reduction method [38]. In this process, 40 mL of an aqueous silver nitrate solution was added dropwise to 100 mL of a sodium borohydride solution under continuous stirring for 2 h. The resulting AgNPs solution was then washed multiple times with distilled water, filtered, and then dried overnight. This process yielded a gray-colored powder as the final AgNPs product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs@CS and CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites were synthesized using the following method: First, 4 mL of a 0.025 mol/L silver nitrate solution was added to 10 mL of a sodium borohydride solution and stirred for 1 h. Then, 18 mL of a CS solution (prepared by dissolving 0.05 g of CS in 18 mL of 1 % acetic acid) was added dropwise to the mixture and stirred for 2.5 h. Subsequently, 40 mL of a CQDs solution was added dropwise, and the resulting mixture was stirred for 3 h. Finally, the mixture was dried at 80 °C, and the obtained dry material was ground into a powder to produce the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites. The synthesis of the CQDs@CS composite follows a similar procedure to that of the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites, with a key difference being the omission of the silver nitrate and sodium borohydride steps. Specifically, after preparing the CS solution by dissolving 0.05 g of CS in 18 mL of 1 % acetic acid, 40 mL of the CQDs solution is added dropwise to the CS solution. The mixture is then stirred for 3 h to ensure proper incorporation of the CQDs into the CS matrix. After stirring, the resulting mixture is dried at 80 °C, and the dried material is ground into a powder to obtain the CQDs@CS composite [39]. The preparation steps of CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites using a hydrothermal method are presented in a schematic diagram in Scheme 1 .

The antioxidant properties of AgNPs, CQDs@CS, and CQDs@AgNPs@CS, were assessed using the DPPH• free radical scavenging assay. This method relies on the ability of DPPH•, a stable free radical, to lose its color in the presence of antioxidants, leading to a decrease in absorbance [40]. In the procedure, 1 mL of a freshly prepared 0.1 mM DPPH• solution was combined with 3 mL of a methanolic solution containing the nanomaterials at concentrations of 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, 500, and 1000 μg/mL. Following thorough mixing, the absorbance was measured at 515 nm after a 30-min incubation in the dark at room temperature, using a UV–Visible spectrophotometer. The results were compared to a control sample of DPPH• without any nanomaterials. To ensure accuracy and reliability, the experiment was conducted three times. The DPPH• scavenging activity was calculated using the following formula [41]:
DPPH • scavenging effect
%
= 1 −
A sample
A control
x
100
Here, Asample represents the absorbance of the sample, while Acontrol denoted the absorbance of the control.

The antibacterial effects of CQDs, AgNPs, CS, CQDs@CS and the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites were evaluated against several microorganisms, including E. coli, K. pneumoniae, A. baumannii, and S. aureus. The bacterial strains used in this study were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) and the Provincial Hospital of Tetouan-Morocco (PHTM). These strains are well-known for their involvement in various skin-related infections.

The antibacterial properties of CQDs, AgNPs, CS, CQDs@CS and the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites were assessed using the disk diffusion technique on Muller-Hinton (MH) agar plates. Following the aromatogram technique described by Vincent [42], bacterial cultures were uniformly spread over the agar surface using a sterile cotton swab. Wells with a diameter of 6 mm were created in the agar, and the CQDs, AgNPs, CS, CQDs@CS and the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanomaterials were introduced into these wells. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and antibacterial activity was determined by measuring the diameters of the inhibition zones surrounding the wells containing the materials [15].

The MIC and MBC of CQDs, AgNPs, CQDs@CS, and CQDs@AgNPs@CS-based nanocomposites were determined using the microtiter plate method along with the MTT assay. In this method, 100 μL of liquid culture medium MH was mixed with 100 μL of each material and serially diluted to various concentrations 15, 7.5, 3.75, 1.875, 0.937, 0.468, 0.234, and 0.117 mg/mL. Subsequently, 10 μL of bacterial suspension was added to each well, and the microplate was incubated at 37 °C for 18 h. After incubation, 10 μL of MTT solution was introduced to each well, followed by an additional 2 h incubation at 37 °C. The appearance of a blue-violet color in the wells indicated active microbial growth. The MIC was defined as the lowest concentration of the nanomaterials that completely inhibited bacterial growth. For the MBC assessment, samples from the MIC test were transferred to agar plates. The MBC was defined as the minimum concentration of the antimicrobial agent that resulted in a 99.9 % reduction in viable colonies compared to the growth control [43].

Male mice, aged 12 to 15 weeks and weighing between 30 and 33 g, were obtained from the animal facility at the Faculty of Sciences, Abdelmalek Essaadi University in Tetouan (Morocco). The mice were housed individually in standard polycarbonate cages under controlled environmental conditions, including a 12-h light-dark cycle and a temperature of 23 °C, with continuous access to food and water. Following an acclimatization period, a total of 42 mice were randomly assigned to seven experimental groups, each consisting of six animals: Group 1 (Untreated Injury), Group 2 (Injury treated with Povidone Iodine), Group 3 (Injury treated with CS), Group 4 (Injury treated with AgNPs), Group 5 (Injury treated with CQDs), Group 6 (Injury treated with CQDs@CS) and Group 7 (Injury treated with CQDs@AgNPs@CS). All animal experiments were conducted in compliance with European regulations on ethical considerations and authorization for animal use, as outlined on February 1, 2013 (NOR: AGRG1238767A). Rigorous measures were taken to minimize both the number of animals used and their potential suffering [44].

To conduct the wound healing test, a mouse incisional wound model was employed. Cutaneous wounds were created as previously outlined [43,45]. Briefly, mice were anesthetized with diethyl ether, and their dorsal fur was shaved. A 1.2 cm incision was made on the back of each mouse. Immediately after wounding, the designated treatment group was topically applied to the affected area to facilitate healing. Group 1 received no treatment, Group 2 treated with Povidone iodine, Group 3 treated CS, Group 4 treated AgNPs, Group 5 with CQDs, Group 6 with CQDs@CS, and Group 7 treated CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites. After recovering from anesthesia, the mice were housed individually in disinfected cages at room temperature. During this period, they were gently handled to reduce stress. Wounds were observed, treated, measured, and photographed daily. The images were transferred to a computer for analysis using ImageJ software, and the wound contraction rate was calculated as the percentage reduction in wound size. Wound contraction (WC) was calculated from the area measurements as follows [46]: C =
Area
D 0
− Area
D t
x
100
Area
D 0
, where t = 3, 6, 9 and 12 days.

To assess the histological changes during the wound healing process, mice were sacrificed, and their wounds are excised on the 1st, 3rd, 6th, 9th, and 12th days post-surgery. The excised wound tissues were fixed in 10 % formalin and processed using standard histological methods, including fixation, dehydration, embedding in paraffin blocks, sectioning into 4 μm-thick slices, and staining with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) [47]. The wound areas were then analyzed histologically. The slides were examined under a light microscope (Olympus BX41) equipped with a Sony DXC 390p camera [44].

Statistical analysis of the experimental data was performed using one-way ANOVA to evaluate differences among groups. Post hoc comparisons were conducted using Tukey's test to identify specific group differences. All experimental values are presented as the mean ± standard error of the mean in the (Table S1, Supplementary Material), with a p-value of <0.05 considered statistically significant (Table S2). The analysis was carried out using SPSS 10.0 for Windows (IBM, Chicago, IL, USA).

The synthetic procedure for the preparation of CQDs, CQDs@CS and CQDs@AgNPs@CS-based nanocomposites is illustrated in Scheme 1. In-depth characterization of these materials, along with the individual components AgNPs and CS provided valuable insight into their structural, morphological, and functional properties.

Comprehensive XRD analysis was conducted to evaluate the crystalline structures of the synthesized materials. The XRD spectrum of the synthesized CQDs, presented in Fig. 1A, confirms their polycrystalline nature, exhibiting a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 23°. This peak indicates the presence of amorphous carbon-based core-shell materials [48]. Additional peaks corresponding to the (002), (100), (102), and (103) crystal planes further suggest associations with graphite and diamond structures within the carbon matrix. The XRD pattern of the AgNPs exhibits three prominent diffraction peaks at 38.05°, 44.25°, and 64.35°, which correspond to the (111), (200), and (220) planes of face-centered cubic silver, as depicted in Fig. 1B. This data is aligns with the Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards (JCPDS) file No. 89.3722 for silver [49]. In contrast, the XRD analysis of pure CS (Fig. 1C) displays a distinctive peak at 2θ = 20°, signifying the high degree of crystallinity characteristic of CS [50]. Incorporating CQDs into CS results in a reduction of the crystalline peak associated with CS while retaining the characteristic peak of the CQDs (Fig. 1D). The spectrum of the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites (Fig. 1E), reveals distinct peaks corresponding to both CQDs and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). This spectrum closely resembles that of the CQDs@CS composite, indicating the presence of CS as well. Additionally, it features peaks at 38.05°, 44.25°, and 64.35°, which correspond to the (111), (200), and (220) crystallographic planes of the face-centered cubic structure of AgNPs, confirming the successful integration of silver nanoparticles into the CQDs@CS matrix, thereby validating the successful synthesis of the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites.

FTIR spectral analysis was performed to investigate the chemical and functional groups present in CQDs, AgNPs, CS, CQDs@CS, and CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites. Fig. 2 provides a comparison of the FTIR spectra of these materials. The FTIR spectrum of synthesized CQDs (Fig. 2A) reveals oxygen-containing functional groups, evidenced by a broad, intense peak in the range of 3100 to 3500 cm−1. The absorption peak at 2941 cm−1 corresponds to CH stretching [51], while the peak at 1598 cm−1 is associated with CO. Bands at 1350 cm−1 and 1259 cm−1 indicate symmetric stretching vibration of COO− [52], and a notable peak at 1031 cm−1 corresponds to C-O-C stretching. Additionally, the peak at 798 cm−1 is attributed to CC functional group [53]. The spectrum for AgNPs (Fig. 2B) appears as a simple straight line without distinct peaks, indicating the absence of organic groups on the surface. The FTIR spectrum of CS (Fig. 2C) exhibits a broad band in the range of 3000 to 3600 cm−1, corresponding to the overlap of OH stretching and NH group vibration [54]. It also displays characteristic bands for free NH2 groups from deacetylated units at 1651 cm−1 and 1585 cm−1, a band at 2875 cm−1 associated with CH stretching and bending vibrations [29,51], and a peak at 1379 cm−1 linked to symmetric CO stretching in CS [51]. Additionally, a peak at 1028 cm−1 is attributed to C-O-C stretching, and a band at 893 cm−1 originates from the pyranose ring [55]. In the FTIR spectrum of CQDs@CS (Fig. 2D), the broad vibrational band in the range of 3000–3600 cm−1 (attributed to NH and OH stretching vibrations) is significantly sharper compared to the spectra of CS and CQDs. The absorption band at 1583 cm−1 (-NH2 bending vibration) shifts to 1573 cm−1, indicating interaction between the -NH2 groups and CQDs. The presence of characteristic peaks from both CQDs and CS in the FTIR spectrum of the CQDs@CS composite confirms their successful synthesis, consistent with previous literature [54]. Notably, the spectra for CQDs@CS and CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites are nearly identical, with only minor changes in peak intensity. This suggests that the introduction of AgNPs did not significantly alter the structure of CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites (Fig. 2E).

The SEM images presented in Fig. 3 illustrate the morphology of CQDs, AgNPs, CS, and their corresponding nanocomposites, including CQDs@CS and CQDs@AgNPs@CS. The SEM analysis of CQDs (Fig. 3A), reveals a near-spherical shape. Similarly, Fig. 3B demonstrates that the AgNPs exhibit a relatively spherical morphology. In contrast, the SEM results for CS, shown (Fig. 3C), highlight a rough surface, characteristic of its polymeric nature. For the CQDs@CS composite (Fig. 3D), the SEM images illustrate a notable change in surface morphology following the incorporation of CQDs into the CS matrix. This includes the emergence of crystal-like structures, potentially resulting from the aggregation of CQDs. Such aggregation can lead to the formation of larger clusters that resemble crystalline shapes, suggesting a complex interplay between the CQDs and the CS matrix. In the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites (Fig. 3E), the SEM images similarly reveal the presence of crystal-like formations, as along with additional spherical structures embedded within the CS matrix, likely corresponding to AgNPs. Since similar crystal-like structures are observed in the CQDs@CS composite due to CQDs aggregation, it is evident that comparable aggregation behaviors may also occur within the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites. These results confirm the successful synthesis of the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites, highlighting the effective integration of its components.

The UV–Vis spectra of CQDs, AgNPs, CS, and their respective composites CQDs@CS and CQDs@AgNPs@CS are presented in Fig. 4 . The UV–Vis spectrum of the CQDs (Fig. 4A) displays a prominent absorption peak at 280 nm, which extends into the visible range. This peak is attributed to π-π* electronic transitions occurring within the aromatic –C=C– bonds of the sp2 hybridized graphitic core [56]. Additionally, the brown-yellow aqueous solution of CQDs exhibits a striking blue luminescence when exposed to ultraviolet light, highlighting their exceptional photoluminescent characteristics. Furthermore, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the synthesized AgNPs (Fig. 4B) reveals a strong peak at 417 nm, corresponding to the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band of AgNPs, thus confirming their successful formation [57]. In these nanoparticles, the overlap of the conduction band and valence band facilitates the free movement of electrons, which contributes to the generation of the plasmon resonance absorption band [58]. The UV–Vis spectrum of CS (Fig. 4C) is characterized by a wide absorption band with moderate intensity at approximately 300 nm [59]. In the CQDs@CS composite, the spectrum reveals characteristic peaks at 283 nm and between 315 and 345 nm (Fig. 4D). The first peak is associated with electronic transitions in the CQDs, while the second peak is attributed to the CS nanoparticles. Importantly, a red shift in λmax is observed for CS, indicating successful attachment to the CQDs. In the spectrum of CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites (Fig. 4E), the peaks observed are consistent with those of CQDs@CS, with no distinct peak for AgNPs. This absence can be attributed to the dominance of the CQDs and CS peaks, which may obscure any contributions from AgNPs. Additionally, interactions among CQDs, CS, and AgNPs could potentially quench or shift the SPR peak, while a lower concentration of AgNPs may dilute their spectral influence. Consequently, the pronounced luminescence and electronic transitions of the CQDs might mask the signals from AgNPs, underscoring the complexity of interactions within these nanocomposites and their implications for optical properties.

The zeta potential is a critical indicator of the stability of colloidal systems, as it reflects the surface charge of nanoparticles and their tendency to aggregate. According to EE Ateia et al. [60], a high magnitude of zeta potential, whether positive or negative, signifies strong electrostatic repulsion, which helps prevent particle aggregation. In our study, zeta potential measurements revealed that the synthesized CQDs exhibited a zeta potential of +19.23 mV, indicating a robust positive surface charge that enhances their stability [61] (Fig. 5A). In contrast, the standalone AgNPs exhibited a negative zeta potential of −7.5 mV (Fig. 5B), which may predispose them to aggregation. The CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites displayed a positive zeta potential of +3.9 mV (Fig. 5C), which can be attributed to the presence of positively charged amine groups in the CS structure [62]. This positive charge contributes to the overall stability of the nanocomposites by promoting electrostatic repulsion among the particles.

The DPPH• radical scavenging assay is the most widely employed method for assessing antioxidant activity in vitro [41]. This technique primarily hinges on the transfer of hydrogen atoms to neutralize free radicals. In this assay, the decolorization of DPPH• is observed as a decrease in absorbance when antioxidants are present. These antioxidants facilitate the conversion of DPPH radicals into a yellow compound, with the extent of this reaction being influenced by the hydrogen-donating ability of the antioxidants.

Fig. 6 illustrates a dose-response bar chart that showcases the percentage of DPPH radical scavenging AgNPs, CQDs@CS, and the nanocomposites of CQDs@AgNPs@CS. The findings reveal that all tested materials demonstrate significant antioxidant capabilities, exhibiting inhibitory effects in a dose-dependent manner. Remarkably, the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites achieved the highest antioxidant activity against DPPH, with a free radical scavenging rate of 80.21 % at a concentration of 1000 μg/mL within 30 min. This performance notably outstripped that of CQDs@CS (74.65 %) and AgNPs (31.89 %). The superior antioxidant efficacy of the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites may be attributed to the enhanced loading of oxidants on the surface of the carbon quantum dots. Supporting these findings, a study by E Hacımüftüoğlu et al. reported similar trends, demonstrating that Ag@M-CS-CQD nanoparticles exhibited greater DPPH radical scavenging activity than M-CQDs and natural extracts like miswak [41]. This alignment with our results underscores the potential of these nanocomposites as effective antioxidant agents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial effect and antimicrobial mechanism of samples
ce:section.ce:para: Since wounds are frequently contaminated by microorganisms, primarily bacteria [63], a study was conducted to evaluate the antibacterial efficacy of CQDs, AgNPs, CS, as well as their composites: CQDs@CS and CQDs@AgNPs@CS, against common wound associated bacteria. The trial utilized both Gram-positive bacterium (S. aureus) and several Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and A. baumannii).

The antibacterial activity was assessed using the disk diffusion method on MH agar plates. Additionally, the MIC of each nanomaterial was determined using microtiter plate method in conjunction with the MTT assay. The zones of inhibition produced by each nanomaterial are presented in Fig. 7 (A) and (B), while the MIC and MBC values are detailed in Table 1 .

The results indicated that CQDs effectively inhibited the growth of E. coli, producing a zone of inhibition of 15 mm and a MIC value of 0.468 mg/mL, with a MBC equivalent to MIC, indicating a bacteriostatic effect. AgNPs demonstrated antibacterial activity against E. coli, S. aureus, and A. baumannii, with inhibition zones measuring 10 mm, 9 mm, and 9 mm respectively, and corresponding MIC and MBC values of 3.75, 7.5, and 3,75 mg/mL. Notably, AgNPs displayed a bactericidal effect against E. coli and A. baumannii, while acting as bacteriostatic towards S. aureus. The CQDs@CS composite produced inhibition zones of 10 mm for the three tested bacteria E. coli, S, aureus, and K. pneumoniae. With MIC and MBC values of 0.937, 7.5, and 3.75 mg/mL respectively. The CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites exhibited enhanced antibacterial activity across all tested strains, with inhibition zones of 14 mm for E. coli, 12 mm for A. baumannii, and 10 mm for S. aureus and K. pneumoniae. Its MIC and MBC values were 0.234, 0.468, 3.75, and 7.5 mg/mL, respectively, indicating a robust bactericidal effect.

These findings can be attributed to the unique antimicrobial properties of CQDs and AgNPs, resulting from the presence of active oxygen radicals in the solution [41,64]. ROS, such as hydroxyl and superoxide radicals, often lead to the degradation of DNA and proteins, ultimately resulting in bacterial cell death [65]. CS, a cationic biopolymer, exhibits antibacterial activity through its interaction with the negatively charged cell walls of bacteria. This interaction disrupts the cell membrane, causing the leakage of intracellular substances and leading to bacterial cell destruction [66]. The incorporation of carbon dots into the polymer matrix significantly enhances its mechanical properties and increases its capacity to generate free radicals. This modification amplifies the physical and chemical reactivity of the polymer, particularly in the formation of ROS, which can inflict oxidative damage by interfering with essential bacterial enzymes, ultimately resulting in cellular disintegration and lysis. Furthermore, employing CS as a coating for metal oxides such as AgNPs mitigates their potential adverse effects, resulting in a sophisticated inorganic-biopolymer nanocomposites that demonstrates increased stability, enhanced antibacterial performance, and a more rapid response compared to unmodified CS [67]. In summary, CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites are proposed to exert strong antibacterial activity through the synergistic interaction of CQDs, AgNPs, and CS. This proposed mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 8 , highlighting the combined contributions of each component to the overall antibacterial effect.

Similar studies have been conducted to investigate the antibacterial effects of Ag/CQD nanocomposite, with comparisons made to unmodified CQDs against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. M Saikia et al. reported significant antibacterial properties of Ag/CQD nanocomposites [68]. While their study primarily highlighted the efficacy of Ag/CQD composites against Gram-positive strains, the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites in our study exhibited not only comparable effectiveness but also superior antibacterial activity against Gram-negative pathogens, including E. coli and A. baumannii. Furthermore, the CS/TiO2NPs/CQDs nanocomposite investigated in other studies has demonstrated robust antibacterial effects, particularly against Gram-negative bacteria [69]. Similar to our findings, this reinforces the notion that our synthesized nanocomposites possess significant antibacterial properties. Overall, our results underscore the superior antibacterial effects of the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites compared to its individual components, suggesting that the synthesized nanocomposites leverage synergistic effects to enhance antibacterial activity against a broad spectrum of pathogens associated with wound infections.

The assessment of the histological state of healing wounds is crucial for effective postoperative patient management [70]. Histological evaluations should encompass the key components of the healing process, including angiogenesis, inflammation, fibroplasia, restoration of the connective tissue matrix, wound contraction and remodeling, epithelialization, and differentiation [7]. The inflammatory response plays a vital role in wound healing, as it prepares the wound environment for repair; however, excessive inflammation can hinder the healing process [4].

Fig. 9 illustrates the histological results of hematoxylin and eosin-stained tissues across all groups (CS group, AgNPs group, CQDs group, CQDs@CS group, and CQDs@AgNPs@CS group) on days 1, 3, 6, 9, and 12. Histological analysis of animals sacrificed 24 h after surgery (day 1) revealed necrosis in the skin tissue at the incision sites due to mechanical damage. The incisional wounds in the untreated, CS, CQDs, CQDs@CS, and CQDs@AgNPs@CS groups were in the inflammatory phase of healing, characterized by a noticeable increase in inflammatory cells density (Fig. S1), particularly neutrophils (indicated by a red triangle). Furthermore, newly formed cells were observed in the superficial layer of the skin (indicated by green arrows). On the third day post-treatment, the untreated and povidone iodine groups exhibited looser connective tissue and more severe inflammation compared to the treated groups. The untreated tissues contained a high number of inflammatory cells, and the wounds were not fully granulated. In contrast, the CS, AgNPs, CQDs, CQDs@CS, and CQDs@AgNPs@CS groups showed fewer inflammatory cells surrounding the wounds, likely due to their effective antibacterial properties [71,72]. By day six, signs of congestion typical of day three persisted, albeit with reduced intensity. On day nine, the presence of new blood vessels (angiogenesis) and increased number of fibroblasts were noted in the CS, AgNPs, CQDs, CQDs@CS, and CQDs@AgNPs@CS groups, indicating that these wounds had progressed into the proliferative phase of repair. The untreated and povidone iodine groups displayed poor re-epithelialization and disorganized granulation tissue. Notably, the CQDs@AgNPs@CS group exhibited greater regularity in both epithelial and connective tissue structures, along with a higher fibroblast count compared to the CS, AgNPs, CQDs, and CQDs@CS groups, which showed similar healing progression. By the twelfth day, the wound healing process had progressed significantly, marked by a reduction in inflammation, the absence of necrosis, reconstructed epithelium, and the formation of granulation tissue across all groups. Notably, the CQDs@AgNPs@CS group exhibited complete epidermal differentiation, full re-epithelialization, and total wound closure (indicated by a yellow star). These results demonstrated that CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites had a remarkable impact on the wound healing process, attributable to the synergistic effect of CQDs, AgNPs, and CS when used together, compared to their individual applications.

To evaluate the impact of CS, AgNPs, CQDs, CQDs@CS, and CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites on tissue repair, macroscopic analyses were performed at several time points (days 1, 3, 6, 9, and 12) using a wound incision model. Fig. 10A and B depicts the progression of wound healing across all groups over the 12-day study period.

On day 1, tissue necrosis and edema were evident around the wound site. By day 3, healing had commenced, with statistical analysis indicating the following rates of wound contraction: 88 % for the untreated group, 72.64 % for the povidone iodine group, and 46.2 %, 44 %, 43.33 %, 40.36 % and 37.17 % for CS, AgNPs, CQDs, CQDs@CS, and CQDs@AgNPs@CS groups, respectively. These results highlighted significant differences in wound closure between the untreated and povidone iodine groups compared to the experimental groups, which exhibited similar healing patterns.

On days 6 and 9, all groups demonstrated improved healing, maintaining their relative ranking in wound contraction rates. By day 12, the wound area had significantly diminished in the treated groups, with contraction percentages of 10 %, 5 %, 3 %, 2 %, and 0.85 % for the CS, AgNPs, CQDs, CQDs@CS, CQDs@AgNPs@CS groups, respectively. In stark contrast, the untreated and povidone iodine groups still exhibited unhealed areas, measuring 51 % and 31 % respectively. Notably, the CQDs@AgNPs@CS group achieved an impressive 99.15 % wound healing effect, underscoring its superior efficacy.

These findings align with previous studies that have examined the role of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) in enhancing wound healing. For instance, research by Frankova et al. demonstrated that AgNPs not only promote fibroblast proliferation but also exhibit antibacterial properties, which can reduce infection rates and facilitate healing [73]. Furthermore, the enhanced performance of the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites in this study can be attributed to the unique properties of its components. Recent findings by E. Hacımüftüoğlu et al. emphasized the significance of multifunctional nanocomposites in tissue engineering. Specifically, Ag@M-CS-CQD gel formulations, which incorporate Ag NPs, CS, and miswak carbon quantum dots (M-CQDs) known for their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, are believed to accelerate wound healing through synergistic effects [41]. Collectively, these studies highlight the potential of nanomaterials in enhancing wound healing strategies, confirming that the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites offer a promising avenue for future research in regenerative medicine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance and comparison of the efficacy of CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites in wound healing and antibacterial activity with other nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs, when combined with AgNPs and CS, form a novel nanocomposites CQDs@AgNPs@CS that have garnered significant attention in the biomedical field. This hybrid material benefits from the unique properties of CQDs, including their excellent optical characteristics and biocompatibility, while leveraging the potent antibacterial effects of AgNPs and the favorable biological properties of CS. Other carbon-based nanomaterials, such as graphene, GO, rGO, CNTs, GQDs, and fullerenes, have also been widely studied for similar purposes. A comparative analysis of the efficacy of CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites in wound healing and antibacterial activity, relative to these other carbon-based nanomaterials, is presented in Table 2 .

The antibacterial activity of CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites demonstrates exceptional potency, surpassing a variety of other formulations. With MICs of 234 μg/mL for E. coli and 3750 μg/mL for S. aureus, CQDs@AgNPs@CS significantly outperforms the CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP composite, which exhibits MICs of 1450 ± 260 μg/mL for E. coli [74]. Furthermore, the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites show a substantially lower MIC than the CS/TiO2NPs/CQDs composite, which has MICs of 12,500 μg/mL for E. coli and 6250 μg/mL for S. aureus [69]. Comparisons with other nanocomposites further highlight the superior antibacterial efficacy of CQDs@AgNPs@CS. For instance, the GO-IONP-Ag composite displays remarkably high MICs of 14,400 μg/mL for both E. coli and S. aureus [75], indicating its reduced antibacterial potency. Similarly, the CQD/Si NP/SF formulation presents MICs of 2000 ± 100 μg/mL for E. coli and 2000 ± 300 μg/mL for S. aureus [16], which, while lower than some other composites, still exceed the potency of CQDs@AgNPs@CS. The Cu2O-TiO2/rGO composite, with MICs of 6250 μg/mL for E. coli and 12,500 μg/mL for S. aureus [76], further exemplifies the variability in antibacterial activity across different nanocomposites. This extensive comparison highlights the superior antibacterial potency of CQDs@AgNPs@CS, establishing it as a prime candidate for a wide range of antimicrobial applications.

Beyond their remarkable antimicrobial properties, a comprehensive investigation of CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites reveals their exceptional efficacy in accelerating wound healing. These innovative nanocomposites achieve an impressive 99.15 % wound closure within just 12 days, positioning them as a leading contender among wound healing agents. In comparison, the PVA/GO-Cu-Cur group demonstrated a lower healing rate of 92.25 % by day 14 [77], while the PVA–CQD/Si NP/SF nanocomposite reported a healing rate of 93.3 % over the same period [16]. Similarly, the combination of PVA with GO/NS/Arg yielded 93.126 % wound healing over the 14-day period [78].

These compelling results not only emphasize the superior performance of CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites but also highlight their transformative potential in wound healing applications. Their rapid and effective outcomes are further complemented by their sustainability; being cost-effective and eco-friendly, derived from waste materials enhances their appeal for widespread adoption. As healthcare increasingly seeks innovative and environmentally responsible treatments, CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites stand poised to drive significant advancements in therapeutic strategies. This approach not only enhances patient recovery rates but also fosters a more sustainable future by utilizing waste materials, ultimately promoting a circular economy within the biomedical sector.

In summary, the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites emerge as a highly promising therapeutic agent for enhancing wound healing and managing infections in clinical practice. This study successfully demonstrated the synthesis and comprehensive characterization of the nanocomposites, confirming its structural integrity and functional properties through advanced techniques such as XRD, FTIR, zeta potential analysis, and SEM. The in vivo results from the incisional wound model in mice highlighted the nanocomposites's ability to enhance wound healing through accelerated re-epithelialization and improved tissue repair. The reduction in inflammatory response, indicated by decreased neutrophil counts, further underscores the therapeutic advantages of this novel formulation. Furthermore, in vitro studies indicated that the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites exhibited excellent antioxidant activity against DPPH•, reaching 80.21 %. Additionally, the antibacterial efficacy of the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites was confirmed by its low MIC and MBC against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative pathogens, including S. aureus, E. coli, A. baumannii, and K. pneumoniae. This broad-spectrum antibacterial activity suggests that the nanocomposites could be an effective solution for preventing and treating infections associated with wounds. Overall, the findings of this study indicate that the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites not only addressed the critical challenges of wound healing and infection management but also represents a significant advancement in the development of multifunctional materials for medical applications.

This study introduces a novel formulation of CQDs, AgNPs, and CS (CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites) that demonstrates significant potential for enhancing wound healing and antibacterial efficacy. By employing both in vivo and in vitro models, the research provides a comprehensive assessment of the nanocomposites' effectiveness against clinically relevant bacterial strains. The use of advanced characterization techniques ensures a robust understanding of their properties. Additionally, the impressive antioxidant activity observed further supports the multifaceted therapeutic potential of these nanocomposites in wound care. However, there are limitations to consider. The in vivo experiments involved a limited sample size, which may impact the generalizability of the findings. The study primarily focuses on short-term outcomes, necessitating additional research to evaluate long-term efficacy and safety. Furthermore, the mechanisms underlying the action of the CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites require additional exploration to fully elucidate the interactions at the molecular level.

Building on the findings of this study, several avenues for future research can be proposed. Longitudinal studies should assess the long-term stability, efficacy, and safety of CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites in various wound healing scenarios, particularly in chronic wounds, diabetic ulcers, and post-surgical wounds. Additionally, exploring the combination of these nanocomposites with other bioactive agents or growth factors could further enhance their therapeutic applications. Investigating targeted delivery systems to improve localization and effectiveness at the wound site, as well as elucidating the molecular mechanisms underlying their antibacterial and healing properties, are also vital directions for future studies. Together, these efforts will help fully realize the therapeutic potential of CQDs@AgNPs@CS nanocomposites in clinical settings.
